title: Linear Reasoning vs. Proof by Cases: Obstacles for Large Language Models in FOL Problem Solving
summary: To comprehensively evaluate the mathematical reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), researchers have introduced abundant mathematical reasoning datasets. However, most existing datasets primarily focus on linear reasoning, neglecting other parts such as proof by contradiction and proof by cases, which are crucial for investigating LLMs' reasoning abilities. To address this limitation, we first introduce a novel first-order logic (FOL) dataset named PC-FOL, annotated by professional mathematicians, focusing on case-based reasoning problems. All instances in this dataset are equipped with a manually written natural language proof, clearly distinguishing it from conventional linear reasoning datasets. Our experimental results over leading LLMs demonstrate a substantial performance gap between linear reasoning and case-based reasoning problems. To further investigate this phenomenon, we provide a theoretical analysis grounded in graphical model, which provides an explanation for the observed disparity between the two types of reasoning problems. We hope this work can reveal the core challenges in the field of automated natural language mathematical proof generation, paving the way for future research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PC-FOL, a novel first-order logic dataset focused on case-based reasoning problems with natural language proofs, demonstrating significant performance gaps between linear and case-based reasoning in leading LLMs, and providing a graphical model-based theoretical explanation for this disparity.
AI.motivation: Existing mathematical reasoning datasets primarily focus on linear reasoning, neglecting crucial reasoning forms such as proof by contradiction and proof by cases that are essential for investigating LLMs' reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: A new first-order logic (FOL) dataset named PC-FOL was created, annotated by professional mathematicians, focusing exclusively on case-based reasoning problems. Each instance includes manually written natural language proofs. The authors also provided a theoretical analysis grounded in graphical models to explain the observed performance differences.
AI.result: Experimental results across leading LLMs reveal a substantial performance gap between linear reasoning and case-based reasoning problems, with case-based reasoning proving significantly more challenging.
AI.conclusion: This work identifies core challenges in automated natural language mathematical proof generation and establishes a foundation for future research to address these limitations.

title: Pipeline for Verifying LLM-Generated Mathematical Solutions
summary: With the growing popularity of Large Reasoning Models and their results in solving mathematical problems, it becomes crucial to measure their capabilities. We introduce a pipeline for both automatic and interactive verification as a more accurate alternative to only checking the answer which is currently the most popular approach for benchmarks. The pipeline can also be used as a generator of correct solutions both in formal and informal languages. 3 AI agents, which can be chosen for the benchmark accordingly, are included in the structure. The key idea is the use of prompts to obtain the solution in the specific form which allows for easier verification using proof assistants and possible use of small models ($\le 8B$). Experiments on several datasets suggest low probability of False Positives. The open-source implementation with instructions on setting up a server is available at https://github.com/LogicEnj/lean4_verification_pipeline.

AI.tldr: A pipeline for verifying Large Reasoning Models' mathematical solutions using prompt-based generation of formal proof assistant inputs, reducing false positives compared to answer-only checking.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models excel at mathematical problem-solving but current benchmarks rely on answer checking alone, which is less accurate. There's a need for more robust verification methods to properly assess these models' capabilities.
AI.method: Introduces a verification pipeline with three selectable AI agents that use prompts to generate solutions in proof assistant-friendly formats. The pipeline supports both automatic and interactive verification, works with small models (≤8B parameters), and can generate correct solutions in formal and informal languages. Open-source implementation available.
AI.result: Experiments across several datasets demonstrate low probability of false positives, validating the pipeline's effectiveness in providing more accurate verification than answer-only approaches.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline offers a practical, accurate alternative to answer checking for benchmarking Large Reasoning Models, successfully reducing false positives while supporting both small and large models.

title: WildGHand: Learning Anti-Perturbation Gaussian Hand Avatars from Monocular In-the-Wild Videos
summary: Despite recent progress in 3D hand reconstruction from monocular videos, most existing methods rely on data captured in well-controlled environments and therefore degrade in real-world settings with severe perturbations, such as hand-object interactions, extreme poses, illumination changes, and motion blur. To tackle these issues, we introduce WildGHand, an optimization-based framework that enables self-adaptive 3D Gaussian splatting on in-the-wild videos and produces high-fidelity hand avatars. WildGHand incorporates two key components: (i) a dynamic perturbation disentanglement module that explicitly represents perturbations as time-varying biases on 3D Gaussian attributes during optimization, and (ii) a perturbation-aware optimization strategy that generates per-frame anisotropic weighted masks to guide optimization. Together, these components allow the framework to identify and suppress perturbations across both spatial and temporal dimensions. We further curate a dataset of monocular hand videos captured under diverse perturbations to benchmark in-the-wild hand avatar reconstruction. Extensive experiments on this dataset and two public datasets demonstrate that WildGHand achieves state-of-the-art performance and substantially improves over its base model across multiple metrics (e.g., up to a $15.8\%$ relative gain in PSNR and a $23.1\%$ relative reduction in LPIPS). Our implementation and dataset are available at https://github.com/XuanHuang0/WildGHand.

AI.tldr: WildGHand is an optimization-based framework for self-adaptive 3D Gaussian splatting on in-the-wild videos that produces high-fidelity hand avatars by disentangling perturbations and using perturbation-aware optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D hand reconstruction methods rely on well-controlled environments and degrade in real-world settings with severe perturbations such as hand-object interactions, extreme poses, illumination changes, and motion blur.
AI.method: WildGHand incorporates two key components: (i) a dynamic perturbation disentanglement module that represents perturbations as time-varying biases on 3D Gaussian attributes during optimization, and (ii) a perturbation-aware optimization strategy that generates per-frame anisotropic weighted masks to guide optimization.
AI.result: WildGHand achieves state-of-the-art performance and substantially improves over its base model, with up to 15.8% relative gain in PSNR and 23.1% relative reduction in LPIPS on extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: The framework identifies and suppresses perturbations across both spatial and temporal dimensions, enabling high-fidelity hand avatar reconstruction from in-the-wild videos. A dataset of monocular hand videos captured under diverse perturbations was curated to benchmark in-the-wild hand avatar reconstruction.

title: Wasserstein Distributionally Robust Online Learning
summary: We study distributionally robust online learning, where a risk-averse learner updates decisions sequentially to guard against worst-case distributions drawn from a Wasserstein ambiguity set centered at past observations. While this paradigm is well understood in the offline setting through Wasserstein Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO), its online extension poses significant challenges in both convergence and computation. In this paper, we address these challenges. First, we formulate the problem as an online saddle-point stochastic game between a decision maker and an adversary selecting worst-case distributions, and propose a general framework that converges to a robust Nash equilibrium coinciding with the solution of the corresponding offline Wasserstein DRO problem. Second, we address the main computational bottleneck, which is the repeated solution of worst-case expectation problems. For the important class of piecewise concave loss functions, we propose a tailored algorithm that exploits problem geometry to achieve substantial speedups over state-of-the-art solvers such as Gurobi. The key insight is a novel connection between the worst-case expectation problem, an inherently infinite-dimensional optimization problem, and a classical and tractable budget allocation problem, which is of independent interest.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses convergence and computational challenges in distributionally robust online learning using Wasserstein ambiguity sets, proposing a general framework that converges to a robust Nash equilibrium and a tailored algorithm for piecewise concave loss functions that achieves substantial speedups.
AI.motivation: Distributionally robust online learning extends offline Wasserstein Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) to sequential decision-making, but faces significant challenges in both convergence and computation when guarding against worst-case distributions drawn from Wasserstein ambiguity sets centered at past observations.
AI.method: The paper formulates the problem as an online saddle-point stochastic game between a decision maker and an adversary selecting worst-case distributions. For piecewise concave loss functions, it proposes a tailored algorithm that exploits problem geometry and establishes a novel connection between the worst-case expectation problem and a tractable budget allocation problem.
AI.result: The proposed general framework converges to a robust Nash equilibrium that coincides with the solution of the corresponding offline Wasserstein DRO problem. The tailored algorithm achieves substantial speedups over state-of-the-art solvers like Gurobi for the important class of piecewise concave loss functions.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses both the theoretical convergence and computational bottlenecks of distributionally robust online learning with Wasserstein ambiguity sets, demonstrating that the problem can be reformulated as an online saddle-point game and solved efficiently through connections to classical budget allocation problems.

title: Surface-based Manipulation Using Tunable Compliant Porous-Elastic Soft Sensing
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures, 1 table, to be published in RoboSoft 2026 proceedings
summary: There is a growing need for soft robotic platforms that perform gentle, precise handling of a wide variety of objects. Existing surface-based manipulation systems, however, lack the compliance and tactile feedback needed for delicate handling. This work introduces the COmpliant Porous-Elastic Soft Sensing (COPESS) integrated with inductive sensors for adaptive object manipulation and localised sensing. The design features a tunable lattice layer that simultaneously modulates mechanical compliance and sensing performance. By adjusting lattice geometry, both stiffness and sensor response can be tailored to handle objects with varying mechanical properties. Experiments demonstrate that by easily adjusting one parameter, the lattice density, from 7 % to 20 %, it is possible to significantly alter the sensitivity and operational force range (about -23x and 9x, respectively). This approach establishes a blueprint for creating adaptive, sensorized surfaces where mechanical and sensory properties are co-optimized, enabling passive, yet programmable, delicate manipulation.

AI.tldr: COPESS integrates inductive sensors with a tunable lattice layer for adaptive object manipulation and localized sensing, enabling simultaneous modulation of mechanical compliance and sensing performance through geometric parameter adjustment.
AI.motivation: There is growing demand for soft robotic platforms capable of gentle, precise handling of diverse objects, but existing surface-based manipulation systems lack the necessary compliance and tactile feedback for delicate operations.
AI.method: The paper introduces COmpliant Porous-Elastic Soft Sensing (COPESS) integrated with inductive sensors, featuring a tunable lattice layer that simultaneously modulates mechanical compliance and sensing performance by adjusting lattice geometry.
AI.result: By varying lattice density from 7% to 20%, sensitivity increases by approximately 23× and operational force range expands by about 9×, demonstrating significant tunability in both mechanical and sensory properties.
AI.conclusion: This approach establishes a blueprint for creating adaptive, sensorized surfaces where mechanical and sensory properties are co-optimized, enabling passive yet programmable delicate manipulation across objects with varying mechanical properties.

title: Evaluating Proactive Risk Awareness of Large Language Models
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in everyday decision-making, their safety responsibilities extend beyond reacting to explicit harmful intent toward anticipating unintended but consequential risks. In this work, we introduce a proactive risk awareness evaluation framework that measures whether LLMs can anticipate potential harms and provide warnings before damage occurs. We construct the Butterfly dataset to instantiate this framework in the environmental and ecological domain. It contains 1,094 queries that simulate ordinary solution-seeking activities whose responses may induce latent ecological impact. Through experiments across five widely used LLMs, we analyze the effects of response length, languages, and modality. Experimental results reveal consistent, significant declines in proactive awareness under length-restricted responses, cross-lingual similarities, and persistent blind spots in (multimodal) species protection. These findings highlight a critical gap between current safety alignment and the requirements of real-world ecological responsibility, underscoring the need for proactive safeguards in LLM deployment.

AI.tldr: A proactive risk awareness evaluation framework for LLMs in the environmental domain, using the Butterfly dataset (1,094 queries) to assess whether LLMs can anticipate potential harms before damage occurs.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become embedded in everyday decision-making, their safety responsibilities must extend beyond reacting to explicit harmful intent to anticipating unintended but consequential risks.
AI.method: Introduced a proactive risk awareness evaluation framework and constructed the Butterfly dataset containing 1,094 queries simulating ordinary solution-seeking activities with potential latent ecological impacts.
AI.result: Experiments across five LLMs reveal consistent, significant declines in proactive awareness under length-restricted responses, cross-lingual similarities, and persistent blind spots in (multimodal) species protection.
AI.conclusion: There is a critical gap between current safety alignment and the requirements of real-world ecological responsibility, underscoring the need for proactive safeguards in LLM deployment.

title: POMDPPlanners: Open-Source Package for POMDP Planning
summary: We present POMDPPlanners, an open-source Python package for empirical evaluation of Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) planning algorithms. The package integrates state-of-the-art planning algorithms, a suite of benchmark environments with safety-critical variants, automated hyperparameter optimization via Optuna, persistent caching with failure recovery, and configurable parallel simulation -- reducing the overhead of extensive simulation studies. POMDPPlanners is designed to enable scalable, reproducible research on decision-making under uncertainty, with particular emphasis on risk-sensitive settings where standard toolkits fall short.

AI.tldr: POMDPPlanners is an open-source Python package for empirical evaluation of POMDP planning algorithms, integrating planning algorithms, benchmark environments, hyperparameter optimization, caching, and parallel simulation to enable scalable, reproducible research on decision-making under uncertainty, especially in risk-sensitive settings.
AI.motivation: Standard toolkits fall short in risk-sensitive settings, and there is a need for scalable, reproducible research on decision-making under uncertainty, particularly for extensive simulation studies that typically have high overhead.
AI.method: The package integrates state-of-the-art planning algorithms, a suite of benchmark environments with safety-critical variants, automated hyperparameter optimization via Optuna, persistent caching with failure recovery, and configurable parallel simulation.
AI.result: The package reduces the overhead of extensive simulation studies, enabling scalable and reproducible research on decision-making under uncertainty with emphasis on risk-sensitive settings.
AI.conclusion: POMDPPlanners is designed to enable scalable, reproducible research on decision-making under uncertainty, particularly in risk-sensitive settings where standard toolkits fall short.

title: AIForge-Doc: A Benchmark for Detecting AI-Forged Tampering in Financial and Form Documents
summary: We present AIForge-Doc, the first dedicated benchmark targeting exclusively diffusion-model-based inpainting in financial and form documents with pixel-level annotation. Existing document forgery datasets rely on traditional digital editing tools (e.g., Adobe Photoshop, GIMP), creating a critical gap: state-of-the-art detectors are blind to the rapidly growing threat of AI-forged document fraud. AIForge-Doc addresses this gap by systematically forging numeric fields in real-world receipt and form images using two AI inpainting APIs -- Gemini 2.5 Flash Image and Ideogram v2 Edit -- yielding 4,061 forged images from four public document datasets (CORD, WildReceipt, SROIE, XFUND) across nine languages, annotated with pixel-precise tampered-region masks in DocTamper-compatible format. We benchmark three representative detectors -- TruFor, DocTamper, and a zero-shot GPT-4o judge -- and find that all existing methods degrade substantially: TruFor achieves AUC=0.751 (zero-shot, out-of-distribution) vs. AUC=0.96 on NIST16; DocTamper achieves AUC=0.563 vs. AUC=0.98 in-distribution, with pixel-level IoU=0.020; GPT-4o achieves only 0.509 -- essentially at chance -- confirming that AI-forged values are indistinguishable to automated detectors and VLMs. These results demonstrate that AIForge-Doc represents a qualitatively new and unsolved challenge for document forensics.

AI.tldr: AIForge-Doc is the first benchmark for diffusion-model-based document inpainting forgery, created by forging numeric fields in real documents using Gemini and Ideogram APIs, revealing that all existing detectors fail against AI-generated forgeries.
AI.motivation: Existing document forgery datasets rely on traditional editing tools (Photoshop, GIMP), leaving state-of-the-art detectors blind to the rapidly growing threat of AI-forged document fraud, creating a critical gap in detection capabilities.
AI.method: Forged numeric fields in real receipts/forms using Gemini 2.5 Flash Image and Ideogram v2 Edit APIs, generating 4,061 images from CORD, WildReceipt, SROIE, XFUND across 9 languages, with pixel-precise tampered-region masks in DocTamper-compatible format.
AI.result: All three benchmarked detectors (TruFor, DocTamper, GPT-4o) perform poorly: TruFor drops from 0.96 to 0.751 AUC (zero-shot), DocTamper drops from 0.98 to 0.563 AUC with IoU=0.020, and GPT-4o achieves 0.509 (chance level).
AI.conclusion: AI-forged document values are indistinguishable to automated detectors and VLMs, making AIForge-Doc a qualitatively new and unsolved challenge for document forensics.

title: $κ$-Explorer: A Unified Framework for Active Model Estimation in MDPs
summary: In tabular Markov decision processes (MDPs) with perfect state observability, each trajectory provides active samples from the transition distributions conditioned on state-action pairs. Consequently, accurate model estimation depends on how the exploration policy allocates visitation frequencies in accordance with the intrinsic complexity of each transition distribution. Building on recent work on coverage-based exploration, we introduce a parameterized family of decomposable and concave objective functions $U_κ$ that explicitly incorporate both intrinsic estimation complexity and extrinsic visitation frequency. Moreover, the curvature $κ$ provides a unified treatment of various global objectives, such as the average-case and worst-case estimation error objectives. Using the closed-form characterization of the gradient of $U_κ$, we propose $κ$-Explorer, an active exploration algorithm that performs Frank-Wolfe-style optimization over state-action occupancy measures. The diminishing-returns structure of $U_κ$ naturally prioritizes underexplored and high-variance transitions, while preserving smoothness properties that enable efficient optimization. We establish tight regret guarantees for $κ$-Explorer and further introduce a fully online and computationally efficient surrogate algorithm for practical use. Experiments on benchmark MDPs demonstrate that $κ$-Explorer provides superior performance compared to existing exploration strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces κ-Explorer, a parameterized family of exploration objectives U_κ and an active exploration algorithm for tabular MDPs that uses Frank-Wolfe optimization over occupancy measures to balance exploration and exploitation based on transition complexity.
AI.motivation: In tabular MDPs with perfect state observability, accurate model estimation depends on how exploration policies allocate visitation frequencies based on the intrinsic complexity of each transition distribution. Existing exploration strategies don't explicitly incorporate this complexity trade-off, making it difficult to balance exploration of high-uncertainty states with sufficient coverage of explored states.
AI.method: The authors introduce a parameterized family of decomposable and concave objective functions U_κ that incorporate both intrinsic estimation complexity and extrinsic visitation frequency. The curvature parameter κ provides a unified treatment of various global objectives (average-case and worst-case estimation error). Using the closed-form gradient characterization, they propose κ-Explorer, which performs Frank-Wolfe-style optimization over state-action occupancy measures, with diminishing-returns structure that prioritizes underexplored and high-variance transitions.
AI.result: κ-Explorer establishes tight regret guarantees and introduces a fully online and computationally efficient surrogate algorithm. Experiments on benchmark MDPs demonstrate that κ-Explorer provides superior performance compared to existing exploration strategies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a principled way to balance exploration based on transition complexity through parameterized objectives, with κ-Explorer offering both theoretical guarantees and empirical advantages over existing methods.

title: Cooperative-Competitive Team Play of Real-World Craft Robots
comment: Accepted by 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026), Vienna, Austria
summary: Multi-agent deep Reinforcement Learning (RL) has made significant progress in developing intelligent game-playing agents in recent years. However, the efficient training of collective robots using multi-agent RL and the transfer of learned policies to real-world applications remain open research questions. In this work, we first develop a comprehensive robotic system, including simulation, distributed learning framework, and physical robot components. We then propose and evaluate reinforcement learning techniques designed for efficient training of cooperative and competitive policies on this platform. To address the challenges of multi-agent sim-to-real transfer, we introduce Out of Distribution State Initialization (OODSI) to mitigate the impact of the sim-to-real gap. In the experiments, OODSI improves the Sim2Real performance by 20%. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experiments with a multi-robot car competitive game and a cooperative task in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a comprehensive robotic system for multi-agent deep RL, proposes efficient training techniques for cooperative and competitive policies, and introduces Out of Distribution State Initialization (OODSI) to improve sim-to-real transfer by 20% in multi-robot experiments.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent deep Reinforcement Learning has advanced game-playing agents, but efficient training of collective robots using multi-agent RL and the transfer of learned policies to real-world applications remain open research questions.
AI.method: Develops a comprehensive robotic system including simulation, distributed learning framework, and physical robot components. Proposes RL techniques for efficient training of cooperative and competitive policies. Introduces Out of Distribution State Initialization (OODSI) to mitigate sim-to-real gap by initializing states differently during training and deployment.
AI.result: OODSI improves Sim2Real performance by 20%. Demonstrated effectiveness through experiments with a multi-robot car competitive game and a cooperative task in real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the challenges of multi-agent sim-to-real transfer, enabling efficient training of cooperative and competitive policies for collective robots in real-world applications.

title: Beyond the Star Rating: A Scalable Framework for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis Using LLMs and Text Classification
summary: Customer-provided reviews have become an important source of information for business owners and other customers alike. However, effectively analyzing millions of unstructured reviews remains challenging. While large language models (LLMs) show promise for natural language understanding, their application to large-scale review analysis has been limited by computational costs and scalability concerns. This study proposes a hybrid approach that uses LLMs for aspect identification while employing classic machine-learning methods for sentiment classification at scale. Using ChatGPT to analyze sampled restaurant reviews, we identified key aspects of dining experiences and developed sentiment classifiers using human-labeled reviews, which we subsequently applied to 4.7 million reviews collected over 17 years from a major online platform. Regression analysis reveals that our machine-labeled aspects significantly explain variance in overall restaurant ratings across different aspects of dining experiences, cuisines, and geographical regions. Our findings demonstrate that combining LLMs with traditional machine learning approaches can effectively automate aspect-based sentiment analysis of large-scale customer feedback, suggesting a practical framework for both researchers and practitioners in the hospitality industry and potentially, other service sectors.

AI.tldr: This study proposes a hybrid approach combining large language models (LLMs) for aspect identification with classic machine learning methods for sentiment classification at scale, applied to 4.7 million restaurant reviews.
AI.motivation: Customer reviews are valuable information sources, but analyzing millions of unstructured reviews is challenging. While LLMs show promise for NLU, their application to large-scale review analysis is limited by computational costs and scalability concerns.
AI.method: The researchers use ChatGPT to identify key aspects from sampled restaurant reviews, develop sentiment classifiers from human-labeled reviews, and apply these classifiers to 4.7 million reviews collected over 17 years from a major online platform.
AI.result: Regression analysis demonstrates that the machine-labeled aspects significantly explain variance in overall restaurant ratings across different aspects of dining experiences, cuisines, and geographical regions.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLMs with traditional machine learning effectively automates aspect-based sentiment analysis of large-scale customer feedback, providing a practical framework for researchers and practitioners in hospitality and other service sectors.

title: Qwen-BIM: developing large language model for BIM-based design with domain-specific benchmark and dataset
summary: As the construction industry advances toward digital transformation, BIM (Building Information Modeling)-based design has become a key driver supporting intelligent construction. Despite Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown potential in promoting BIM-based design, the lack of specific datasets and LLM evaluation benchmarks has significantly hindered the performance of LLMs. Therefore, this paper addresses this gap by proposing: 1) an evaluation benchmark for BIM-based design together with corresponding quantitative indicators to evaluate the performance of LLMs, 2) a method for generating textual data from BIM and constructing corresponding BIM-derived datasets for LLM evaluation and fine-tuning, and 3) a fine-tuning strategy to adapt LLMs for BIM-based design. Results demonstrate that the proposed domain-specific benchmark effectively and comprehensively assesses LLM capabilities, highlighting that general LLMs are still incompetent for domain-specific tasks. Meanwhile, with the proposed benchmark and datasets, Qwen-BIM is developed and achieves a 21.0% average increase in G-Eval score compared to the base LLM model. Notably, with only 14B parameters, performance of Qwen-BIM is comparable to that of general LLMs with 671B parameters for BIM-based design tasks. Overall, this study develops the first domain-specific LLM for BIM-based design by introducing a comprehensive benchmark and high-quality dataset, which provide a solid foundation for developing BIM-related LLMs in various fields.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the first domain-specific LLM for BIM-based design by introducing a comprehensive evaluation benchmark, high-quality BIM-derived datasets, and a fine-tuning strategy. The proposed Qwen-BIM model (14B parameters) achieves a 21.0% average increase in G-Eval score compared to base models, with performance comparable to 671B parameter general LLMs for BIM tasks.
AI.motivation: While LLMs show potential in BIM-based design, the lack of domain-specific datasets and evaluation benchmarks has significantly hindered their performance. This study aims to bridge this gap by developing comprehensive evaluation frameworks and high-quality datasets specifically for BIM-based design tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose three main contributions: (1) an evaluation benchmark for BIM-based design with quantitative indicators, (2) a method for generating textual data from BIM models to construct BIM-derived datasets, and (3) a fine-tuning strategy to adapt LLMs for BIM-based design. The Qwen-BIM model is developed using these components.
AI.result: The proposed benchmark effectively and comprehensively assesses LLM capabilities, revealing that general LLMs are still inadequate for domain-specific tasks. Qwen-BIM achieves a 21.0% average increase in G-Eval score compared to base LLM models. Remarkably, with only 14B parameters, Qwen-BIM performs comparably to 671B parameter general LLMs in BIM-based design tasks.
AI.conclusion: This study successfully develops the first domain-specific LLM for BIM-based design by introducing a comprehensive benchmark and high-quality dataset. These contributions provide a solid foundation for developing BIM-related LLMs in various fields and demonstrate that domain-specific fine-tuning can significantly enhance model performance with substantially fewer parameters.

title: An interactive enhanced driving dataset for autonomous driving
summary: The evolution of autonomous driving towards full automation demands robust interactive capabilities; however, the development of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models is constrained by the sparsity of interactive scenarios and inadequate multimodal alignment in existing data. To this end, this paper proposes the Interactive Enhanced Driving Dataset (IEDD). We develop a scalable pipeline to mine million-level interactive segments from naturalistic driving data based on interactive trajectories, and design metrics to quantify the interaction processes. Furthermore, the IEDD-VQA dataset is constructed by generating synthetic Bird's Eye View (BEV) videos where semantic actions are strictly aligned with structured language. Benchmark results evaluating ten mainstream Vision Language Models (VLMs) are provided to demonstrate the dataset's reuse value in assessing and fine-tuning the reasoning capabilities of autonomous driving models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Interactive Enhanced Driving Dataset (IEDD), a resource addressing sparse interactive scenarios and multimodal alignment limitations in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model development for autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: The evolution toward full automation in autonomous driving requires robust interactive capabilities, but current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model development faces constraints due to sparse interactive scenarios and inadequate multimodal alignment in existing data.
AI.method: The researchers propose a scalable pipeline to mine million-level interactive segments from naturalistic driving data based on interactive trajectories and design metrics to quantify interaction processes. They construct the IEDD-VQA dataset by generating synthetic Bird's Eye View (BEV) videos with strictly aligned semantic actions and structured language descriptions.
AI.result: Benchmark evaluations of ten mainstream Vision Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate the dataset's utility for assessing and fine-tuning the reasoning capabilities of autonomous driving models.
AI.conclusion: The IEDD provides a valuable resource that addresses critical data limitations in VLA model development, enabling more effective training and evaluation of interactive driving scenarios.

title: CITED: A Decision Boundary-Aware Signature for GNNs Towards Model Extraction Defense
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have demonstrated superior performance in various applications, such as recommendation systems and financial risk management. However, deploying large-scale GNN models locally is particularly challenging for users, as it requires significant computational resources and extensive property data. Consequently, Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) has become increasingly popular, offering a convenient way to deploy and access various models, including GNNs. However, an emerging threat known as Model Extraction Attacks (MEAs) presents significant risks, as adversaries can readily obtain surrogate GNN models exhibiting similar functionality. Specifically, attackers repeatedly query the target model using subgraph inputs to collect corresponding responses. These input-output pairs are subsequently utilized to train their own surrogate models at minimal cost. Many techniques have been proposed to defend against MEAs, but most are limited to specific output levels (e.g., embedding or label) and suffer from inherent technical drawbacks. To address these limitations, we propose a novel ownership verification framework CITED which is a first-of-its-kind method to achieve ownership verification on both embedding and label levels. Moreover, CITED is a novel signature-based method that neither harms downstream performance nor introduces auxiliary models that reduce efficiency, while still outperforming all watermarking and fingerprinting approaches. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our CITED framework. Code is available at: https://github.com/LabRAI/CITED.

AI.tldr: CITED is a novel signature-based ownership verification framework for graph neural networks that works on both embedding and label levels, outperforming existing watermarking and fingerprinting approaches without harming downstream performance or introducing auxiliary models.
AI.motivation: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are powerful but challenging to deploy locally due to resource requirements. While MLaaS offers convenience, it faces Model Extraction Attacks (MEAs) where adversaries can train surrogate GNN models by querying the target model. Existing defense techniques are limited to specific output levels and suffer from technical drawbacks.
AI.method: CITED proposes a first-of-its-kind ownership verification framework that operates on both embedding and label levels using a signature-based approach. Unlike watermarking or fingerprinting, CITED does not harm downstream model performance or introduce auxiliary models that reduce efficiency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that CITED outperforms all existing watermarking and fingerprinting approaches, showing both effectiveness and robustness against model extraction attacks.
AI.conclusion: CITED provides a comprehensive solution for verifying GNN ownership that addresses limitations of existing methods, offering protection on both embedding and label levels without compromising model performance or efficiency. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/LabRAI/CITED.

title: Prompt-Level Distillation: A Non-Parametric Alternative to Model Fine-Tuning for Efficient Reasoning
summary: Advanced reasoning typically requires Chain-of-Thought prompting, which is accurate but incurs prohibitive latency and substantial test-time inference costs. The standard alternative, fine-tuning smaller models, often sacrifices interpretability while introducing significant resource and operational overhead. To address these limitations, we introduce Prompt-Level Distillation (PLD). We extract explicit reasoning patterns from a Teacher model and organize them into a structured list of expressive instructions for the Student model's System Prompt. Evaluated on the StereoSet and Contract-NLI datasets using Gemma-3 4B, PLD improved Macro F1 scores from 57\% to 90.0\% and 67\% to 83\% respectively, enabling this compact model to match frontier performance with negligible latency overhead. These expressive instructions render the decision-making process transparent, allowing for full human verification of logic, making this approach ideal for regulated industries such as law, finance, and content moderation, as well as high-volume use cases and edge devices.

AI.tldr: Prompt-Level Distillation (PLD) extracts reasoning patterns from a Teacher model and organizes them into structured instructions for a Student model's system prompt, enabling the Student to match frontier performance with negligible latency overhead while maintaining interpretability.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-Thought prompting is accurate but incurs prohibitive latency and test-time inference costs. Fine-tuning smaller models sacrifices interpretability while introducing significant resource and operational overhead.
AI.method: Prompt-Level Distillation (PLD) extracts explicit reasoning patterns from a Teacher model and organizes them into a structured list of expressive instructions for the Student model's System Prompt.
AI.result: Evaluated on StereoSet and Contract-NLI datasets using Gemma-3 4B, PLD improved Macro F1 scores from 57% to 90.0% and 67% to 83% respectively, matching frontier performance with negligible latency overhead.
AI.conclusion: The expressive instructions render the decision-making process transparent, allowing for full human verification of logic, making this approach ideal for regulated industries (law, finance, content moderation), high-volume use cases, and edge devices.

title: Pressure Reveals Character: Behavioural Alignment Evaluation at Depth
summary: Evaluating alignment in language models requires testing how they behave under realistic pressure, not just what they claim they would do. While alignment failures increasingly cause real-world harm, comprehensive evaluation frameworks with realistic multi-turn scenarios remain lacking. We introduce an alignment benchmark spanning 904 scenarios across six categories -- Honesty, Safety, Non-Manipulation, Robustness, Corrigibility, and Scheming -- validated as realistic by human raters. Our scenarios place models under conflicting instructions, simulated tool access, and multi-turn escalation to reveal behavioural tendencies that single-turn evaluations miss. Evaluating 24 frontier models using LLM judges validated against human annotations, we find that even top-performing models exhibit gaps in specific categories, while the majority of models show consistent weaknesses across the board. Factor analysis reveals that alignment behaves as a unified construct (analogous to the g-factor in cognitive research) with models scoring high on one category tending to score high on others. We publicly release the benchmark and an interactive leaderboard to support ongoing evaluation, with plans to expand scenarios in areas where we observe persistent weaknesses and to add new models as they are released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a comprehensive alignment benchmark with 904 realistic multi-turn scenarios across six categories, finding that even top language models exhibit alignment gaps and that alignment behaviors form a unified construct similar to the g-factor in cognitive research.
AI.motivation: Evaluating alignment in language models requires testing realistic behavioral pressure, not just claims. While alignment failures increasingly cause real-world harm, comprehensive evaluation frameworks with realistic multi-turn scenarios remain lacking.
AI.method: The researchers introduced an alignment benchmark with 904 scenarios across six categories (Honesty, Safety, Non-Manipulation, Robustness, Corrigibility, and Scheming). Scenarios involve conflicting instructions, simulated tool access, and multi-turn escalation. They evaluated 24 frontier models using LLM judges validated against human annotations, and performed factor analysis to examine alignment relationships across categories.
AI.result: Even top-performing models exhibit gaps in specific alignment categories, and the majority show consistent weaknesses across the board. Factor analysis reveals that alignment behaves as a unified construct - models scoring high on one category tend to score high on others, analogous to the g-factor in cognitive research.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark and interactive leaderboard are publicly released to support ongoing evaluation, with plans to expand scenarios in areas of persistent weakness and add new models as they are released.

title: Efficient and Explainable End-to-End Autonomous Driving via Masked Vision-Language-Action Diffusion
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have emerged as promising candidates for end-to-end autonomous driving. However, these models typically face challenges in inference latency, action precision, and explainability. Existing autoregressive approaches struggle with slow token-by-token generation, while prior diffusion-based planners often rely on verbose, general-purpose language tokens that lack explicit geometric structure. In this work, we propose Masked Vision-Language-Action Diffusion for Autonomous Driving (MVLAD-AD), a novel framework designed to bridge the gap between efficient planning and semantic explainability via a masked vision-language-action diffusion model. Unlike methods that force actions into the language space, we introduce a discrete action tokenization strategy that constructs a compact codebook of kinematically feasible waypoints from real-world driving distributions. Moreover, we propose geometry-aware embedding learning to ensure that embeddings in the latent space approximate physical geometric metrics. Finally, an action-priority decoding strategy is introduced to prioritize trajectory generation. Extensive experiments on nuScenes and derived benchmarks demonstrate that MVLAD-AD achieves superior efficiency and outperforms state-of-the-art autoregressive and diffusion baselines in planning precision, while providing high-fidelity and explainable reasoning.

AI.tldr: MVLAD-AD proposes a masked vision-language-action diffusion model for autonomous driving that introduces discrete action tokenization with kinematically feasible waypoints, geometry-aware embeddings, and action-priority decoding to achieve efficient, precise, and explainable trajectory planning, outperforming existing autoregressive and diffusion baselines on nuScenes.
AI.motivation: LLMs and VLMs for autonomous driving face challenges in inference latency, action precision, and explainability. Autoregressive approaches suffer from slow token-by-token generation, while diffusion-based planners rely on verbose language tokens lacking explicit geometric structure, limiting both efficiency and precision.
AI.method: The paper introduces MVLAD-AD, a masked vision-language-action diffusion framework with three key innovations: (1) discrete action tokenization constructing a compact codebook of kinematically feasible waypoints from real-world driving distributions; (2) geometry-aware embedding learning ensuring latent space embeddings approximate physical geometric metrics; (3) action-priority decoding strategy to prioritize trajectory generation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on nuScenes and derived benchmarks demonstrate that MVLAD-AD achieves superior efficiency and outperforms state-of-the-art autoregressive and diffusion baselines in planning precision, while providing high-fidelity and explainable reasoning.
AI.conclusion: MVLAD-AD successfully bridges the gap between efficient planning and semantic explainability in autonomous driving by combining discrete action tokenization, geometry-aware embeddings, and prioritized decoding within a diffusion framework, demonstrating both computational efficiency and planning precision compared to existing methods.

title: CREDIT: Certified Ownership Verification of Deep Neural Networks Against Model Extraction Attacks
summary: Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) has emerged as a widely adopted paradigm for providing access to deep neural network (DNN) models, enabling users to conveniently leverage these models through standardized APIs. However, such services are highly vulnerable to Model Extraction Attacks (MEAs), where an adversary repeatedly queries a target model to collect input-output pairs and uses them to train a surrogate model that closely replicates its functionality. While numerous defense strategies have been proposed, verifying the ownership of a suspicious model with strict theoretical guarantees remains a challenging task. To address this gap, we introduce CREDIT, a certified ownership verification against MEAs. Specifically, we employ mutual information to quantify the similarity between DNN models, propose a practical verification threshold, and provide rigorous theoretical guarantees for ownership verification based on this threshold. We extensively evaluate our approach on several mainstream datasets across different domains and tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Our implementation is publicly available at: https://github.com/LabRAI/CREDIT.

AI.tldr: MLaaS services are vulnerable to Model Extraction Attacks (MEAs) where adversaries extract models through repeated queries. CREDIT is a certified ownership verification framework using mutual information to quantify model similarity, providing rigorous theoretical guarantees against MEAs with state-of-the-art performance across diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: MLaaS services enable convenient access to DNN models through standardized APIs, but are highly vulnerable to Model Extraction Attacks (MEAs). While numerous defense strategies have been proposed, there remains a critical gap in verifying model ownership with strict theoretical guarantees. Existing approaches lack the formal verification framework needed to confidently identify suspicious models.
AI.method: CREDIT employs mutual information to quantify the similarity between DNN models, proposes a practical verification threshold, and provides rigorous theoretical guarantees for ownership verification based on this threshold. The framework enables certified ownership verification against MEAs by establishing a mathematically sound approach to comparing models.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation across multiple mainstream datasets across different domains and tasks demonstrates state-of-the-art performance. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/LabRAI/CREDIT.
AI.conclusion: CREDIT successfully addresses the gap in certified ownership verification against MEAs by providing a theoretically grounded approach with practical applicability, achieving superior performance compared to existing methods. This enables MLaaS providers to confidently verify model ownership and detect unauthorized model extraction attempts.

title: HALO: A Unified Vision-Language-Action Model for Embodied Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong performance in robotic manipulation, but often struggle in long-horizon or out-of-distribution scenarios due to the lack of explicit mechanisms for multimodal reasoning and anticipating how the world will evolve under action. Recent works introduce textual chain-of-thought or visual subgoal prediction within VLA models to reason, but still fail to offer a unified human-like reasoning framework for joint textual reasoning, visual foresight, and action prediction. To this end, we propose HALO, a unified VLA model that enables embodied multimodal chain-of-thought (EM-CoT) reasoning through a sequential process of textual task reasoning, visual subgoal prediction for fine-grained guidance, and EM-CoT-augmented action prediction. We instantiate HALO with a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture that decouples semantic reasoning, visual foresight, and action prediction into specialized experts while allowing seamless cross-expert collaboration. To enable HALO learning at scale, we introduce an automated pipeline to synthesize EM-CoT training data along with a carefully crafted training recipe. Extensive experiments demonstrate that: (1) HALO achieves superior performance in both simulated and real-world environments, surpassing baseline policy pi_0 by 34.1% on RoboTwin benchmark; (2) all proposed components of the training recipe and EM-CoT design help improve task success rate; and (3) HALO exhibits strong generalization capabilities under aggressive unseen environmental randomization with our proposed EM-CoT reasoning.

AI.tldr: HALO is a unified Vision-Language-Action model that enables embodied multimodal chain-of-thought (EM-CoT) reasoning through sequential textual task reasoning, visual subgoal prediction, and EM-CoT-augmented action prediction using a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language-Action models struggle in long-horizon or out-of-distribution scenarios due to lack of explicit mechanisms for multimodal reasoning and anticipating world evolution under action. Existing works (textual chain-of-thought or visual subgoal prediction) fail to provide a unified human-like reasoning framework for joint textual reasoning, visual foresight, and action prediction.
AI.method: HALO uses a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture that decouples semantic reasoning, visual foresight, and action prediction into specialized experts with seamless cross-expert collaboration. It enables embodied multimodal chain-of-thought (EM-CoT) reasoning through sequential process: textual task reasoning → visual subgoal prediction for fine-grained guidance → EM-CoT-augmented action prediction. An automated pipeline synthesizes EM-CoT training data with a carefully crafted training recipe.
AI.result: HALO surpasses baseline policy pi_0 by 34.1% on RoboTwin benchmark. All proposed components (training recipe and EM-CoT design) improve task success rate. HALO exhibits strong generalization capabilities under aggressive unseen environmental randomization with EM-CoT reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HALO framework demonstrates that a unified embodied multimodal chain-of-thought approach enables superior performance in robotic manipulation across both simulated and real-world environments. The sequential reasoning framework combining textual task reasoning, visual subgoal prediction, and EM-CoT-augmented action prediction significantly enhances task success rates and generalization capabilities.

title: PVminer: A Domain-Specific Tool to Detect the Patient Voice in Patient Generated Data
summary: Patient-generated text such as secure messages, surveys, and interviews contains rich expressions of the patient voice (PV), reflecting communicative behaviors and social determinants of health (SDoH). Traditional qualitative coding frameworks are labor intensive and do not scale to large volumes of patient-authored messages across health systems. Existing machine learning (ML) and natural language processing (NLP) approaches provide partial solutions but often treat patient-centered communication (PCC) and SDoH as separate tasks or rely on models not well suited to patient-facing language. We introduce PVminer, a domain-adapted NLP framework for structuring patient voice in secure patient-provider communication. PVminer formulates PV detection as a multi-label, multi-class prediction task integrating patient-specific BERT encoders (PV-BERT-base and PV-BERT-large), unsupervised topic modeling for thematic augmentation (PV-Topic-BERT), and fine-tuned classifiers for Code, Subcode, and Combo-level labels. Topic representations are incorporated during fine-tuning and inference to enrich semantic inputs. PVminer achieves strong performance across hierarchical tasks and outperforms biomedical and clinical pre-trained baselines, achieving F1 scores of 82.25% (Code), 80.14% (Subcode), and up to 77.87% (Combo). An ablation study further shows that author identity and topic-based augmentation each contribute meaningful gains. Pre-trained models, source code, and documentation will be publicly released, with annotated datasets available upon request for research use.

AI.tldr: PVminer is a domain-adapted NLP framework for structuring patient voice in secure patient-provider communication, using patient-specific BERT encoders combined with unsupervised topic modeling to detect PV at Code, Subcode, and Combo levels.
AI.motivation: Patient-generated text contains rich expressions of patient voice reflecting communicative behaviors and social determinants of health. Traditional qualitative coding is labor-intensive and doesn't scale to large volumes of patient-authored messages. Existing ML/NLP approaches either treat patient-centered communication and SDoH as separate tasks or use models not well suited to patient-facing language.
AI.method: PVminer formulates PV detection as a multi-label, multi-class prediction task integrating: (1) patient-specific BERT encoders (PV-BERT-base and PV-BERT-large), (2) unsupervised topic modeling for thematic augmentation (PV-Topic-BERT), and (3) fine-tuned classifiers for Code, Subcode, and Combo-level labels. Topic representations are incorporated during fine-tuning and inference to enrich semantic inputs.
AI.result: PVminer achieves strong performance across hierarchical tasks, outperforming biomedical and clinical pre-trained baselines with F1 scores of 82.25% (Code), 80.14% (Subcode), and up to 77.87% (Combo). Ablation study shows that author identity and topic-based augmentation each contribute meaningful performance gains.
AI.conclusion: PVminer demonstrates superior performance for structuring patient voice across hierarchical classification tasks. Pre-trained models, source code, and documentation will be publicly released, with annotated datasets available upon request for research use.

title: Diagnosing Causal Reasoning in Vision-Language Models via Structured Relevance Graphs
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) achieve strong performance on visual question answering benchmarks, yet often rely on spurious correlations rather than genuine causal reasoning. Existing evaluations primarily assess the correctness of the answers, making it unclear whether failures arise from limited reasoning capability or from misidentifying causally relevant information. We introduce Vision-Language Causal Graphs (VLCGs), a structured, query-conditioned representation that explicitly encodes causally relevant objects, attributes, relations, and scene-grounded assumptions. Building on this representation, we present ViLCaR, a diagnostic benchmark comprising tasks for Causal Attribution, Causal Inference, and Question Answering, along with graph-aligned evaluation metrics that assess relevance identification beyond final answer accuracy. Experiments in state-of-the-art LVLMs show that injecting structured relevance information significantly improves attribution and inference consistency compared to zero-shot and standard in-context learning. These findings suggest that current limitations in LVLM causal reasoning stem primarily from insufficient structural guidance rather than a lack of reasoning capacity.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Vision-Language Causal Graphs (VLCGs) and ViLCaR benchmark to diagnose and improve causal reasoning in LVLMs by explicitly encoding causally relevant information and evaluating models beyond final answer accuracy.
AI.motivation: LVLMs achieve strong performance on visual question answering benchmarks but often rely on spurious correlations rather than genuine causal reasoning. Existing evaluations primarily assess answer correctness, making it unclear whether failures stem from limited reasoning capability or from misidentifying causally relevant information.
AI.method: Introduces Vision-Language Causal Graphs (VLCGs), a structured, query-conditioned representation that explicitly encodes causally relevant objects, attributes, relations, and scene-grounded assumptions. Presents ViLCaR, a diagnostic benchmark with tasks for Causal Attribution, Causal Inference, and Question Answering, along with graph-aligned evaluation metrics assessing relevance identification beyond final answer accuracy.
AI.result: Experiments on state-of-the-art LVLMs demonstrate that injecting structured relevance information significantly improves attribution and inference consistency compared to zero-shot and standard in-context learning.
AI.conclusion: Current limitations in LVLM causal reasoning stem primarily from insufficient structural guidance rather than a lack of reasoning capacity.

title: PropFly: Learning to Propagate via On-the-Fly Supervision from Pre-trained Video Diffusion Models
comment: The first two authors contributed equally to this work (equal contribution)
summary: Propagation-based video editing enables precise user control by propagating a single edited frame into following frames while maintaining the original context such as motion and structures. However, training such models requires large-scale, paired (source and edited) video datasets, which are costly and complex to acquire. Hence, we propose the PropFly, a training pipeline for Propagation-based video editing, relying on on-the-Fly supervision from pre-trained video diffusion models (VDMs) instead of requiring off-the-shelf or precomputed paired video editing datasets. Specifically, our PropFly leverages one-step clean latent estimations from intermediate noised latents with varying Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) scales to synthesize diverse pairs of 'source' (low-CFG) and 'edited' (high-CFG) latents on-the-fly. The source latent serves as structural information of the video, while the edited latent provides the target transformation for learning propagation. Our pipeline enables an additional adapter attached to the pre-trained VDM to learn to propagate edits via Guidance-Modulated Flow Matching (GMFM) loss, which guides the model to replicate the target transformation. Our on-the-fly supervision ensures the model to learn temporally consistent and dynamic transformations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our PropFly significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on various video editing tasks, producing high-quality editing results.

AI.tldr: PropFly is a training pipeline for propagation-based video editing that uses on-the-fly supervision from pre-trained video diffusion models (VDMs) instead of requiring large paired datasets. It leverages one-step clean latent estimations from intermediate noised latents with varying Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) scales to synthesize diverse source and edited latents, then trains an adapter via Guidance-Modulated Flow Matching (GMFM) loss to propagate edits temporally.
AI.motivation: Propagation-based video editing enables precise user control by propagating a single edited frame into following frames while maintaining original context. However, training such models requires large-scale, paired (source and edited) video datasets, which are costly and complex to acquire.
AI.method: PropFly leverages on-the-fly supervision from pre-trained VDMs instead of requiring off-the-shelf or precomputed paired video editing datasets. The key components are: (1) leveraging one-step clean latent estimations from intermediate noised latents with varying Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) scales to synthesize diverse pairs of 'source' (low-CFG) and 'edited' (high-CFG) latents on-the-fly; (2) using Guidance-Modulated Flow Matching (GMFM) loss to train an adapter attached to the pre-trained VDM to learn to propagate edits; (3) the source latent serves as structural information, while the edited latent provides the target transformation for learning propagation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that PropFly significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on various video editing tasks, producing high-quality editing results.
AI.conclusion: PropFly provides an efficient training pipeline for propagation-based video editing by eliminating the need for large-scale paired datasets. The on-the-fly supervision ensures temporally consistent and dynamic transformations, making video editing more accessible and practical.

title: GauS: Differentiable Scheduling Optimization via Gaussian Reparameterization
summary: Efficient operator scheduling is a fundamental challenge in software compilation and hardware synthesis. While recent differentiable approaches have sought to replace traditional ones like exact solvers or heuristics with gradient-based search, they typically rely on categorical distributions that fail to capture the ordinal nature of time and suffer from a parameter space that scales poorly. In this paper, we propose a novel differentiable framework, GauS, that models operator scheduling as a stochastic relaxation using Gaussian distributions, which fully utilize modern parallel computing devices like GPUs. By representing schedules as continuous Gaussian variables, we successfully capture the ordinal nature of time and reduce the optimization space by orders of magnitude. Our method is highly flexible to represent various objectives and constraints, which provides the first differentiable formulation for the complex pipelined scheduling problem. We evaluate our method on a range of benchmarks, demonstrating that Gaus achieves Pareto-optimal results.

title: ActionReasoning: Robot Action Reasoning in 3D Space with LLM for Robotic Brick Stacking
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, accepted by the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation
summary: Classical robotic systems typically rely on custom planners designed for constrained environments. While effective in restricted settings, these systems lack generalization capabilities, limiting the scalability of embodied AI and general-purpose robots. Recent data-driven Vision-Language-Action (VLA) approaches aim to learn policies from large-scale simulation and real-world data. However, the continuous action space of the physical world significantly exceeds the representational capacity of linguistic tokens, making it unclear if scaling data alone can yield general robotic intelligence. To address this gap, we propose ActionReasoning, an LLM-driven framework that performs explicit action reasoning to produce physics-consistent, prior-guided decisions for robotic manipulation. ActionReasoning leverages the physical priors and real-world knowledge already encoded in Large Language Models (LLMs) and structures them within a multi-agent architecture. We instantiate this framework on a tractable case study of brick stacking, where the environment states are assumed to be already accurately measured. The environmental states are then serialized and passed to a multi-agent LLM framework that generates physics-aware action plans. The experiments demonstrate that the proposed multi-agent LLM framework enables stable brick placement while shifting effort from low-level domain-specific coding to high-level tool invocation and prompting, highlighting its potential for broader generalization. This work introduces a promising approach to bridging perception and execution in robotic manipulation by integrating physical reasoning with LLMs.

AI.tldr: ActionReasoning is an LLM-driven framework that leverages multi-agent architectures and physical priors to perform explicit action reasoning for physics-consistent robotic manipulation decisions, demonstrated successfully in a brick stacking case study.
AI.motivation: Classical robotic planners are environment-specific and lack generalization, while existing data-driven VLA approaches struggle with the continuous action space of the physical world that exceeds the representational capacity of linguistic tokens. The key question is whether scaling data alone can yield general robotic intelligence.
AI.method: ActionReasoning implements a multi-agent LLM architecture that serializes environment states and generates physics-aware action plans by leveraging the physical priors and real-world knowledge already encoded in LLMs. The framework performs explicit action reasoning to produce prior-guided, physics-consistent decisions for robotic manipulation.
AI.result: The framework enables stable brick placement in a tractable case study, demonstrating that it can shift effort from low-level domain-specific coding to high-level tool invocation and prompting. The approach shows potential for broader generalization beyond the specific task.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces a promising approach to bridging perception and execution in robotic manipulation by integrating physical reasoning with LLMs, suggesting that leveraging existing LLM knowledge through explicit action reasoning could enable more generalizable robotic systems.

title: On Data Engineering for Scaling LLM Terminal Capabilities
summary: Despite rapid recent progress in the terminal capabilities of large language models, the training data strategies behind state-of-the-art terminal agents remain largely undisclosed. We address this gap through a systematic study of data engineering practices for terminal agents, making two key contributions: (1) Terminal-Task-Gen, a lightweight synthetic task generation pipeline that supports seed-based and skill-based task construction, and (2) a comprehensive analysis of data and training strategies, including filtering, curriculum learning, long context training, and scaling behavior. Our pipeline yields Terminal-Corpus, a large-scale open-source dataset for terminal tasks. Using this dataset, we train Nemotron-Terminal, a family of models initialized from Qwen3(8B, 14B, 32B) that achieve substantial gains on Terminal-Bench 2.0: Nemotron-Terminal-8B improves from 2.5% to 13.0% Nemotron-Terminal-14B improves from 4.0% to 20.2%, and Nemotron-Terminal-32B improves from 3.4% to 27.4%, matching the performance of significantly larger models. To accelerate research in this domain, we open-source our model checkpoints and most of our synthetic datasets at https://huggingface.co/collections/nvidia/nemotron-terminal.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Terminal-Task-Gen, a synthetic task generation pipeline and Terminal-Corpus dataset for terminal agents, training Nemotron-Terminal models (8B/14B/32B) that significantly outperform previous models on Terminal-Bench 2.0.
AI.motivation: Despite rapid progress in terminal capabilities of large language models, the training data strategies behind state-of-the-art terminal agents remain largely undisclosed.
AI.method: Terminal-Task-Gen is a lightweight synthetic task generation pipeline supporting seed-based and skill-based task construction. The authors also conducted a comprehensive analysis of data and training strategies including filtering, curriculum learning, long context training, and scaling behavior.
AI.result: The pipeline yields Terminal-Corpus, a large-scale open-source dataset. Training Nemotron-Terminal models (initialized from Qwen3 8B, 14B, 32B) on this dataset yields substantial gains: 8B improves from 2.5% to 13.0%, 14B from 4.0% to 20.2%, and 32B from 3.4% to 27.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, matching the performance of significantly larger models.
AI.conclusion: The authors open-source model checkpoints and synthetic datasets to accelerate research in terminal agent domain, demonstrating that systematic data engineering practices can significantly improve terminal agent performance.

title: Predicting Sentence Acceptability Judgments in Multimodal Contexts
summary: Previous work has examined the capacity of deep neural networks (DNNs), particularly transformers, to predict human sentence acceptability judgments, both independently of context, and in document contexts. We consider the effect of prior exposure to visual images (i.e., visual context) on these judgments for humans and large language models (LLMs). Our results suggest that, in contrast to textual context, visual images appear to have little if any impact on human acceptability ratings. However, LLMs display the compression effect seen in previous work on human judgments in document contexts. Different sorts of LLMs are able to predict human acceptability judgments to a high degree of accuracy, but in general, their performance is slightly better when visual contexts are removed. Moreover, the distribution of LLM judgments varies among models, with Qwen resembling human patterns, and others diverging from them. LLM-generated predictions on sentence acceptability are highly correlated with their normalised log probabilities in general. However, the correlations decrease when visual contexts are present, suggesting that a higher gap exists between the internal representations of LLMs and their generated predictions in the presence of visual contexts. Our experimental work suggests interesting points of similarity and of difference between human and LLM processing of sentences in multimodal contexts.

AI.tldr: This study examines how visual context affects sentence acceptability judgments in humans and LLMs, comparing it to the effects of textual context.
AI.motivation: Previous research has explored DNNs and transformers for sentence acceptability prediction independently and in document contexts, but the impact of visual context remains understudied.
AI.method: The authors test the effect of prior exposure to visual images on sentence acceptability judgments for humans and various LLMs, comparing performance with and without visual contexts.
AI.result: Visual images have minimal impact on human acceptability ratings, but LLMs exhibit the "compression effect" seen in document contexts. LLM predictions correlate with log probabilities, but this correlation decreases with visual contexts. Different LLMs show varying distribution patterns, with Qwen resembling human patterns.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals interesting similarities and differences between human and LLM processing of sentences in multimodal contexts, suggesting that visual contexts affect LLMs differently than textual contexts.

title: Long-Term Multi-Session 3D Reconstruction Under Substantial Appearance Change
summary: Long-term environmental monitoring requires the ability to reconstruct and align 3D models across repeated site visits separated by months or years. However, existing Structure-from-Motion (SfM) pipelines implicitly assume near-simultaneous image capture and limited appearance change, and therefore fail when applied to long-term monitoring scenarios such as coral reef surveys, where substantial visual and structural change is common. In this paper, we show that the primary limitation of current approaches lies in their reliance on post-hoc alignment of independently reconstructed sessions, which is insufficient under large temporal appearance change. We address this limitation by enforcing cross-session correspondences directly within a joint SfM reconstruction. Our approach combines complementary handcrafted and learned visual features to robustly establish correspondences across large temporal gaps, enabling the reconstruction of a single coherent 3D model from imagery captured years apart, where standard independent and joint SfM pipelines break down. We evaluate our method on long-term coral reef datasets exhibiting significant real-world change, and demonstrate consistent joint reconstruction across sessions in cases where existing methods fail to produce coherent reconstructions. To ensure scalability to large datasets, we further restrict expensive learned feature matching to a small set of likely cross-session image pairs identified via visual place recognition, which reduces computational cost and improves alignment robustness.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenge of reconstructing and aligning 3D models across long-term environmental monitoring scenarios (e.g., coral reef surveys) where large temporal appearance changes occur. Existing SfM pipelines fail because they assume near-simultaneous image capture and limited appearance change.
AI.motivation: Existing Structure-from-Motion (SfM) pipelines implicitly assume near-simultaneous image capture and limited appearance change, which fails in long-term monitoring scenarios where substantial visual and structural change is common. The primary limitation lies in their reliance on post-hoc alignment of independently reconstructed sessions, which is insufficient under large temporal appearance change.
AI.method: The authors enforce cross-session correspondences directly within a joint SfM reconstruction. Their approach combines complementary handcrafted and learned visual features to robustly establish correspondences across large temporal gaps. To ensure scalability, they restrict expensive learned feature matching to a small set of likely cross-session image pairs identified via visual place recognition.
AI.result: Evaluated on long-term coral reef datasets with significant real-world change, the method demonstrates consistent joint reconstruction across sessions in cases where existing methods fail to produce coherent reconstructions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enables the reconstruction of a single coherent 3D model from imagery captured years apart, where standard independent and joint SfM pipelines break down, addressing the core limitation of post-hoc alignment in long-term monitoring scenarios.

title: Imputation of Unknown Missingness in Sparse Electronic Health Records
summary: Machine learning holds great promise for advancing the field of medicine, with electronic health records (EHRs) serving as a primary data source. However, EHRs are often sparse and contain missing data due to various challenges and limitations in data collection and sharing between healthcare providers. Existing techniques for imputing missing values predominantly focus on known unknowns, such as missing or unavailable values of lab test results; most do not explicitly address situations where it is difficult to distinguish what is missing. For instance, a missing diagnosis code in an EHR could signify either that the patient has not been diagnosed with the condition or that a diagnosis was made, but not shared by a provider. Such situations fall into the paradigm of unknown unknowns. To address this challenge, we develop a general purpose algorithm for denoising data to recover unknown missing values in binary EHRs. We design a transformer-based denoising neural network where the output is thresholded adaptively to recover values in cases where we predict data are missing. Our results demonstrate improved accuracy in denoising medical codes within a real EHR dataset compared to existing imputation approaches and leads to increased performance on downstream tasks using the denoised data. In particular, when applying our method to a real world application, predicting hospital readmission from EHRs, our method achieves statistically significant improvement over all existing baselines.
